summary: Cantrill demonstrates how DTrace can be used to diagnose GNOME desktop startup performance issues by tracing I/O operations and CPU sampling during login. His analysis reveals that GConf's XML backend accounts for over a quarter of all I/O, shared object loading causes a 'death of a thousand cuts,' and symlink resolution alone may cost a full second of startup time. The post serves as both a practical debugging tutorial and an advertisement for DTrace's capabilities in understanding complex system behavior.
key_points: GConf's XML backend causes over 25% of all I/O during GNOME startup
Shared object text and read-only data loading accounts for nearly a third of I/O
Symlink chasing during startup (nearly 80 symlinks) may add a full second of blocked I/O time
CPU utilization is dwarfed by I/O costs as the main performance bottleneck
Icon file loading is another significant I/O contributor, addressed in GTK+ 2.6
money_quotes: This is classic death-of-a-thousand-cuts, and it's hard to see that there's an easy way to fix this.
Over a quarter of all I/O is from GConf – and many of these are from wandering around an expansive directory hierarchy looking for configuration information.
CPU utilization doesn't look like a big issue – or it's one that is at least dwarfed by the cost of performing I/O.
We might be spending as much as one second of GNOME startup blocked on I/O just to chase symlinks! Ouch!

Having an app link to libpango-1.0.so.0 and not libpango-1.0.so.400.1 is clearly a Good Thing, and having this be a symlink instead of a hardlink is clearly a Good Thing – but all of that goodness leaves you with a read dependency that's hard to work around.
key_insight: DTrace's ability to trace I/O and CPU activity system-wide reveals that GNOME startup slowness stems primarily from scattered filesystem reads rather than CPU usage, with GConf and symlink resolution being major culprits.
